summary: Apple made the entire first episode of Silo available as a promoted tweet on Twitter, an unprecedented move enabled by Twitter's new long-form video feature. Gruber finds the promotional strategy interesting but questions the optics of Apple bestowing prestige on Musk's increasingly toxic platform. While acknowledging Twitter's undeniable reach as justification, he argues the partnership benefits Twitter more than Apple — giving it premium brand credibility it desperately needs. He concludes the arrangement isn't outrageous but feels skeevy.
key_points: Apple shared a full TV episode natively on Twitter rather than linking to its own app or YouTube, a novel promotional strategy only possible since Twitter enabled two-hour video uploads
The move highlights the tension between Twitter's still-massive reach and Musk's increasingly toxic stewardship of the platform
Gruber argues Twitter gains more from the partnership than Apple does — it's not money but prestige that Twitter lacks, and Apple is lending it some
Apple's free premiere strategy across all its shows is smart customer acquisition, but embedding directly on Twitter raises questions other advertising placements don't
money_quotes: The biggest obstacle to any streaming service is just getting someone to try it.
Asking anyone today to put aside the politics of Twitter is a bit like the old 'But other than that, how'd you enjoy the play, Mrs. Lincoln?' joke.
Advertising is fundamentally about paying to reach an audience, not an endorsement of content, but at some level it's a partnership.
The only place where I see anyone asking 'Why is Apple advertising there?' is Twitter.
It's not the money (which Musk has plenty of), it's the prestige (of which Twitter is by most accounts bankrupt).
You can't buy prestige, and Apple has chosen to bestow some on Twitter through this promotion.
Is it outrageous that Apple continues to advertise on Twitter? I say no. But it feels a bit skeevy, and more than a little curious, that they choose to.
key_insight: Apple's Silo promotion on Twitter isn't outrageous, but it reveals an asymmetric partnership where Apple lends prestige to a platform that desperately needs it more than Apple needs the reach.
